narrative: As a white male applicant with a part-time enrollment plan and a lower-middle family income background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on past students' data to predict bar exam success on the first try. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.30 (below the typical 3.21 average), LSAT score of 31 (well under the 36.70 average), and school deciles of 3rd in Year 1 and 1st in Year 3 (both lagging behind averages around 5.5)—and calculated a 48% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This elevated risk threshold led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting students predicted to pass confidently. The most influential factor pushing the model toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking in the bottom tier, which strongly signaled potential struggles in advanced coursework compared to peers who ranked much higher. Counterbalancing this somewhat was your family income bracket, which nudged the prediction slightly toward success, as students from similar lower-middle backgrounds have sometimes outperformed expectations. Your LSAT score provided another modest push toward passing, though its below-average level limited its positive impact relative to top performers. Overall, your academic deciles, especially in later years, carried the heaviest weight in overriding these mitigating factors, setting you apart from typical admitted students who show stronger, more consistent grade rankings. While your part-time status is uncommon (only about 7% of students pursue it), it didn't dominate the decision here. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male applicant—many from similar demographics succeed with better academic momentum—but the model highlighted gaps that mirrored patterns in students who later failed the bar. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above average, strengthening your later-year grades through targeted improvement or additional coursework, and exploring programs that support part-time students with financial aid tailored to lower-middle income families. Reapplying after addressing these core areas could shift your predicted odds significantly, opening doors to approval.
